presentation_title: SS08: Education, Simulation and Assessment - Proficiency-Based Preparation Significantly Improves FES Certification Performance

frame_analyses.description: Presentation introduces study on FES (Fundamentals of Endoscopy) certification preparation methods

Discusses FES certification requirement starting 2018, exam structure (cognitive and manual skills), and manual skills components tested (navigation, loop reduction, retroflexion, mucosal evaluation, targeting)
Reports 40% pass rate at presenter's institution vs 68% overall, with failures on both cognitive and manual sections. Notes >65 colonoscopies needed to pass.
Describes curriculum development using FES didactic materials and benchtop models (MITIE trainers) - inexpensive, reproducible, targeted at difficult skills
Shows physical benchtop trainer models with endoscopy simulator screen integration for skills practice
Outlines methodology: enrollment → initial survey → video training + pre-test → residents train to proficiency → FES exam + post survey
15 residents (6 PGY-4, 9 PGY-5; 9 male, 6 female; mostly right-handed) across specialties; 56 colonoscopies, 39 endoscopies performed

frame_analyses.content: Pre- and Post-Test Manual Skills Scoring
frame_analyses.description: Graph showing improvement across 5 manual skills (Navigation, Loop Reduction, Retroflexion, Mucosal Evaluation, Targeting) with post-test scores higher than pre-test

Loop reduction identified as weakest skill; training did not significantly improve it. Kyoto model not found useful; 47% of residents rated models as 'Not Very Useful' or 'Not At All Useful'. Future training plans: GI Mentor colonoscopy simulation.
Development of custom didactic curriculum with SCORE modules in PGY 1-3 years (EGD, Colonoscopy) and FES didactic curriculum in PGY 4-5 years
Survey shows 47% thought they passed FES but 87% actually passed - indicates need for objective feedback. Demonstrates poor self-assessment by residents.
FES post-test skills score trended but did not significantly correlate with colonoscopy numbers (r=1.91, p=0.079). 67% of residents passed despite not meeting >65 colonoscopy threshold.

cannot_score: This is an educational presentation/lecture, not a recorded FLS task performance. No procedural task is being performed that can be scored against FLS rubrics.

teaching_content_extracted.presentation_focus: Demonstrates efficacy of proficiency-based simulation preparation for FES certification
key_study_findings: Benchtop model training (MITIE) improved manual skills scores from pre-test to post-test across all 5 domains
87% actual pass rate in residents who received structured preparation vs 40% baseline at institution
Loop reduction was most resistant skill to improvement
Case numbers (colonoscopies) alone did not correlate with certification success; structured training essential
Resident self-assessment was poor (47% predicted pass, 87% actual pass)
curriculum_implications: Early SCORE modules in PGY 1-3 with didactic and simulation
Dedicated FES preparation curriculum in PGY 4-5
Benchtop trainer models as cost-effective teaching tools
Virtual simulation (GI Mentor) identified as improvement for loop reduction training
Objective feedback critical due to poor self-handicapping by residents
teaching_content_extracted.methodology_relevance_to_fls: Study addresses FES manual skills certification which parallels FLS MISTELS task objectives (navigation, tissue manipulation, procedural competency). Demonstrates that deliberate practice with validated models improves objective task-based assessment scores.

scoring_not_applicable: Video classification as 'instructional' means this presentation is not scorable under FLS rubrics. It is a peer-reviewed research presentation describing educational outcomes, not a performance recording of an FLS task (Peg Transfer, Pattern Cut, Endoloop, Extracorporeal Suture, or Intracorporeal Suture). No completion time, penalties, or technical performance data can be extracted because no procedure is being performed.
confidence_rationale: Video content is clearly identifiable as a conference presentation (SAGES 2017) with title slides, speaker name, institution, research methodology, and results discussion. Zero ambiguity regarding classification.
